en.extract:
Whitehead's broadbill (Calyptomena whiteheadi) is a species of bird in the family Calyptomenidae. It is endemic to the mountain ranges of north-central Borneo, where it mainly inhabits montane forests and forest edges at elevations of 900–1,700 m (3,000–5,600 ft). It is 24–27 cm (9.4–10.6 in) long, with males weighing 142–171 g (5.0–6.0 oz) and females weighing 150–163 g (5.3–5.7 oz). Males are vivid green and have a black throat patch, black spots on the ear-coverts and back of the neck, and black markings and streaking all over the body. The tails and flight feathers are also blackish. Females are smaller and lack the black markings on the head and underparts. Juveniles look similar to adults but have fewer black markings.
Described by the British ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe in 1887, Whitehead's broadbill is named after the British explorer John Whitehead. It mainly feeds on fruit and supplements its diet with insects. Breeding probably occurs from March to June, with clutches containing one or two eggs. Although it is classified as being of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature, it is threatened by habitat destruction and its population is thought to be decreasing.


Taxonomy and systematics
Whitehead's broadbill was described as Calyptomena whiteheadi by the British naturalist Richard Bowdler Sharpe in 1887 based on specimens from Mount Kinabalu, Borneo. The name of the genus, Calyptomena, is from the Ancient Greek words kaluptos, meaning covered, and mēnē, meaning moon. The specific name, whiteheadi, is in honour of the British explorer John Whitehead, who collected the specimens based on which the species was described. Whitehead's broadbill is the official common name designated by the International Ornithologists' Union. Other names for the species include Whitehead's green broadbill, black-throated broadbill, and black-throated green broadbill.
Whitehead's broadbill is one of three species in the genus Calyptomena, a genus of three bright green broadbills found in Southeast Asia. Calyptomena is one of two genera in the family Calyptomenidae, the other being Smithornis, a genus of three rather dull-coloured species found in Africa. Although species-level relationships within the family are unclear, both the genera are monophyletic (including all descendants of a common ancestor) taxa that are sister (most closely related) to each other.


Description

The largest species in the genus Calyptomena, Whitehead's broadbill is 24–27 cm (9.4–10.6 in) long, with males weighing 142–171 g (5.0–6.0 oz) and females weighing 150–163 g (5.3–5.7 oz). Males are vivid green, with some black on the top of the head, black spots on the ear-coverts and back of the neck, and a large black patch on the lower throat and upper breast. They also have extensive black markings on the wings and upper back, conspicuous streaking on the underparts, and blackish tails and flight feathers. The iris is dark brown, the feet are olive to greyish-green, and the bill is black with a pale lower mandible. The forehead has a large tuft of feathers that nearly covers the bill. Females are smaller than males, with a smaller forehead tuft, no black marking on the head, and duller green underparts that lack black streaking. Juveniles are similar to adults but have fewer black markings.
Whitehead's broadbill can be told apart from the other species in its genus by its larger size, black throat, and more extensive black markings on the body.


Vocalisations
Snoring, grinding, and wheezing two-syllable calls such as toc-trrr are characteristic of Whitehead's broadbill. Groups of 3–4 males make a rough, harsh kerrrrrr or kh-khrrrrrr, along with a chek, rrrrt-rrrrt and teek-waaaaarrr. Other calls include a staccato eek-eek-eek, a harsh tzip followed by a 2–3 second long rattle, and a fast series of harsh notes given in flight, all of which are similar to those made by woodpeckers. A hissing ee-ooo may be the species' alarm call. It also makes a flat, low-pitched go-up similar to that of a trogon (which attracts individuals when mimicked), a high saaat, a loud, grating call like that of a jay, a snore-like wheeze, and other hissing and grating sounds.


Distribution and habitat
Whitehead's broadbill is endemic to Borneo, where it is found in the northern and central parts of the island. It is likely found throughout the entire range of mountains that runs down the centre of the island from Mount Kinabalu to Kayan Mentarang and Mount Batu Timbang, although it is locally absent from some areas.
It inhabits primary montane forest and forest edge, preferring areas with taller growth. It is most common at elevations of 900–1,700 m (3,000–5,600 ft), although it can be found at elevations as low as 600 m (2,000 ft) and as high as 1,980 m (6,500 ft). It does not migrate, but does move locally depending on the fruiting seasons of the trees it feeds on. Individuals have also been seen at 75 m (246 ft) during severe droughts. 


Behaviour and ecology
Whitehead's broadbills usually perch silently, but are sometimes loud and conspicuous. Their generation length is 4.2 years.


Feeding
The species mainly feeds on fruit, supplementing its diet with insects. The fruits eaten vary from small berries to large drupes, with fruits up to 15 mm × 20 mm (0.59 in × 0.79 in) in size found in stomachs. It has also been observed feeding on the strong-smelling fruit of Litsea cubica. In Kinabalu Park, the species has been observed hunting moths near lights at dawn.
Whitehead's broadbills usually forage alone, but can sometimes be found in small, noisy flocks near fruiting trees. They also occasionally join mixed-species foraging flocks, which can also contain Sunda cuckooshrikes, chestnut-hooded laughingthrushes, Sunda laughingthrushes, and Whitehead's trogons.


Breeding
The breeding season of Whitehead's broadbill probably lasts from March to June. Nests are suspended from slender branches around 15 m (50 ft) above the ground, with the outside made of fresh green moss. The inside is firm and lined with dry bamboo leaves, forming a well-sheltered cavity. A long extending "tail" made of lichen and moss helps camouflage the nest. Clutches contain one or two eggs, which are glossy and pale yellow in colour. The eggs measure 1.26 in–1.45 in × 1 in (32 mm–37 mm × 25 mm). Incubation lasts 19 days and fledging period is 27 days.


Status
Whitehead's broadbill is classified as being of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature due to its sufficiently large range and population and a lack of significant population decline. It is fairly common in appropriate habitat and occurs in several protected areas, such as Gunung Mulu National Park and Kinabalu Park. However, its population is currently thought to be declining due to habitat destruction.


References

'''Whitehead's broadbill''' ('''''Calyptomena whiteheadi''''') is a [[species]] of bird in the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Calyptomenidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the mountain ranges of north-central [[Borneo]], where it mainly inhabits [[montane forest]]s and [[forest edge]]s at elevations of {{cvt|900–1700|m|ft}}. It is {{cvt|24–27|cm|in}} long, with males weighing {{cvt|142–171|g|oz}} and females weighing {{cvt|150–163|g|oz}}. Males are vivid green and have a black throat patch, black spots on the {{Birdgloss|ear-coverts}} and back of the neck, and black markings and streaking all over the body. The tails and [[flight feather]]s are also blackish. Females are smaller and lack the black markings on the head and {{Birdgloss|underparts}}. Juveniles look similar to adults but have fewer black markings.

Described by the British [[ornithologist]] [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] in 1887, Whitehead's broadbill is named after the British explorer [[John Whitehead (explorer)|John Whitehead]]. It mainly feeds on fruit and supplements its diet with insects. Breeding probably occurs from March to June, with [[Clutch (eggs)|clutches]] containing one or two eggs. Although it is classified as being of [[Least-concern species|least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]], it is threatened by [[habitat destruction]] and its population is thought to be decreasing.

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
Whitehead's broadbill was described as ''Calyptomena whiteheadi'' by the British naturalist [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] in 1887 based on specimens from [[Mount Kinabalu]], [[Borneo]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Sharpe |first=Richard Bowdler |date=1887 |title=On a new species of ''Calytomena'' |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/91212#page/692/mode/1up |journal=[[Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London]] |language=en |location=London |publisher=[[Academic Press]] |page=558 |lccn=86640225 |oclc=1779524 |via=[[Biodiversity Heritage Library]] |access-date=2022-04-09 |archive-date=2022-03-03 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220303114732/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/91212#page/692/mode/1up |url-status=live }}</ref> The name of the [[genus]], ''[[Calyptomena]]'', is from the [[Ancient Greek]] words ''kaluptos'', meaning covered, and ''mēnē'', meaning moon. The [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]], ''whiteheadi'', is in honour of the British explorer [[John Whitehead (explorer)|John Whitehead]],<ref>{{Cite book |last=Jobling |first=James A. |title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names |publisher=[[Christopher Helm Publishers|Christopher Helm]] |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |location=London |pages=86, 407 |language=en |oclc=1040808348}}</ref> who collected the specimens based on which the species was described.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lecroy |first1=Mary |last2=Sloss |first2=Richard |date=2000 |title=Type specimens of birds in the American Museum of Natural History. Part 3, Passeriformes: Eurylaimidae, Dendrocolaptidae, Furnariidae, Formicariidae, Conopophagidae, and Rhinocryptidae |url=https://digitallibrary.amnh.org/handle/2246/1607 |journal=[[Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History]] |series=257 |volume=3 |page=6 |hdl=2246/1607 |issn=0003-0090 |oclc=1132125195 |access-date=2022-04-09 |archive-date=2022-03-03 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220303114740/https://digitallibrary.amnh.org/handle/2246/1607 |url-status=live }}</ref> Whitehead's broadbill is the official [[common name]] designated by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]].<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |editor-last=Donsker |editor-first=David |editor2-last=Gill |editor2-first=Frank |editor3-last=Rasmussen |editor3-first=Pamela |title=NZ wrens, Sapayoa, asities, broadbills, pittas |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/nz_wrens/ |access-date=2022-03-03 |website=IOC World Bird List |language=en-US |archive-date=2020-11-16 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201116163353/https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/nz_wrens/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Other names for the species include Whitehead's green broadbill, black-throated broadbill, and black-throated green broadbill.<ref>{{Cite web |title=''Calyptomena whiteheadi'' (Whitehead's Broadbill) |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=406E9C46C1EBBDA3 |access-date=2022-03-04 |website=[[Avibase]] |archive-date=2021-12-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211224232443/https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=406E9C46C1EBBDA3 |url-status=live }}</ref>

Whitehead's broadbill is one of three species in the genus ''Calyptomena'', a genus of three bright green broadbills found in [[Southeast Asia]]. ''Calyptomena'' is one of two genera in the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Calyptomenidae]], the other being ''[[Smithornis]]'', a genus of three rather dull-coloured species found in Africa. Although species-level relationships within the family are unclear, both the genera are [[monophyletic]] (including all descendants of a common ancestor) taxa that are [[Sister group|sister]] (most closely related) to each other.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Winkler |first1=David W. |title=African and Green Broadbills (Calyptomenidae) |date=2020-03-04 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/calypt1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |editor-last=Billerman |editor-first=Shawn M. |publisher=[[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]] |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.calypt1.01 |access-date=2022-03-26 |last2=Billerman |first2=Shawn M. |last3=Lovette |first3=Irby J. |s2cid=216172460 |editor2-last=Keeney |editor2-first=Brooke K. |editor3-last=Rodewald |editor3-first=Paul G. |editor4-last=Schulenberg |editor4-first=Thomas S. |archive-date=2022-03-26 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220326095409/https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/calypt1/1.0/introduction |url-status=live |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Whitehead's Broadbill, Kinabalu Park, Sabah, Malaysia.jpg|left|thumb|Whitehead's broadbill in [[Kinabalu Park]]]]
The largest species in the genus ''Calyptomena'', Whitehead's broadbill is {{cvt|24–27|cm|in}} long, with males weighing {{cvt|142–171|g|oz}} and females weighing {{cvt|150–163|g|oz}}. Males are vivid green, with some black on the top of the head, black spots on the {{Birdgloss|ear-coverts}} and back of the neck, and a large black patch on the lower throat and upper breast. They also have extensive black markings on the wings and upper back, conspicuous streaking on the {{Birdgloss|underparts}}, and blackish tails and [[flight feather]]s. The [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]] is dark brown, the feet are olive to greyish-green, and the bill is black with a pale lower {{Birdgloss|mandible}}. The forehead has a large tuft of feathers that nearly covers the bill. Females are smaller than males, with a smaller forehead tuft, no black marking on the head, and duller green underparts that lack black streaking. Juveniles are similar to adults but have fewer black markings.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Bruce |first=Murray D. |date=2020-03-04 |editor-last=Billerman |editor-first=Shawn M. |editor2-last=Keeney |editor2-first=Brooke K. |editor3-last=Rodewald |editor3-first=Paul G. |editor4-last=Schulenberg |editor4-first=Thomas S. |title=Whitehead's Broadbill (''Calyptomena whiteheadi'') |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/whibro1/1.0/introduction |language=en |publisher=[[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]] |doi=10.2173/bow.whibro1.01 |url-access=subscription |access-date=2022-03-04 |journal=Birds of the World |s2cid=216367826 |archive-date=2022-03-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220304092809/https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/whibro1/1.0/introduction |url-status=live }}</ref>

Whitehead's broadbill can be told apart from the other species in its genus by its larger size, black throat, and more extensive black markings on the body.<ref name=":0" />

=== Vocalisations ===
Snoring, grinding, and wheezing two-syllable calls such as ''toc-trrr'' are characteristic of Whitehead's broadbill. Groups of 3–4 males make a rough, harsh ''kerrrrrr'' or ''kh-khrrrrrr'', along with a ''chek, rrrrt-rrrrt'' and ''teek-waaaaarrr''. Other calls include a [[staccato]] ''eek-eek-eek'', a harsh ''tzip'' followed by a 2–3 second long rattle, and a fast series of harsh notes given in flight, all of which are similar to those made by [[woodpecker]]s.<ref name=":0" /> A hissing ''ee-ooo'' may be the species' [[alarm call]]. It also makes a flat, low-pitched ''go-up'' similar to that of a [[trogon]] (which attracts individuals when mimicked), a high ''saaat'', a loud, grating call like that of a [[jay]], a snore-like wheeze, and other hissing and grating sounds.<ref name=":0" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
Whitehead's broadbill is [[Endemism|endemic]] to Borneo, where it is found in the northern and central parts of the island. It is likely found throughout the entire range of mountains that runs down the centre of the island from Mount Kinabalu to [[Kayan Mentarang National Park|Kayan Mentarang]] and [[Mount Batu Timbang]], although it is locally absent from some areas.<ref name=":0" />

It inhabits [[Primary forest|primary]] [[montane forest]] and [[forest edge]], preferring areas with taller growth. It is most common at elevations of {{cvt|900–1700|m|ft}}, although it can be found at elevations as low as {{cvt|600|m|ft}} and as high as {{cvt|1980|m|ft}}. It does not [[Bird migration|migrate]], but does move locally depending on the fruiting seasons of the trees it feeds on. Individuals have also been seen at {{cvt|75|m|ft}} during severe droughts.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":12" /> 

== Behaviour and ecology ==
Whitehead's broadbills usually perch silently, but are sometimes loud and conspicuous.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last=Myers |first=Susan |url= |title=Birds of Borneo: Sabah, Sarawak, Brunei and Kalimantan |publisher=[[Christopher Helm Publishers|Christopher Helm]] |others=Illustrated by Richard Allen, Hilary Burn, Clive Byers, Daniel Cole, John Cox, Anthony Disley, Alan Harris, Szabolcs Kokay, Mike Langman, Ian Lewington, Andrew Mackay, Stephen Message, Christopher Schmidt, Jan Wilczur, and Tim Worfolk |year=2016 |isbn=978-1-4729-2444-5 |edition=Second |location=London |page=202 |language=en |oclc=944318084}}</ref> Their [[Generation time|generation length]] is 4.2 years.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

=== Feeding ===
The species mainly feeds on fruit, supplementing its diet with insects. The fruits eaten vary from small berries to large [[drupe]]s, with fruits up to {{cvt|15 × 20|mm|in}} in size found in stomachs. It has also been observed feeding on the strong-smelling fruit of ''[[Litsea cubica]]''. In [[Kinabalu Park]], the species has been observed hunting moths near lights at dawn.<ref name=":0" />

Whitehead's broadbills usually forage alone, but can sometimes be found in small, noisy flocks near fruiting trees.<ref name=":0" /> They also occasionally join [[mixed-species foraging flock]]s,<ref name=":0" /> which can also contain [[Sunda cuckooshrike]]s, [[chestnut-hooded laughingthrush]]es, [[Sunda laughingthrush]]es, and [[Whitehead's trogon]]s.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=van Els |first1=Paul |last2=Chua |first2=Vivienne L. |last3=Burner |first3=Ryan C. |last4=Rahman |first4=Mustafa Abdul |last5=Sheldon |first5=Frederick H. |date=2016-05-13 |title=Notes on the life history of ''Harpactes whiteheadi'' (Aves: Trogonidae), with a description of the juvenile plumage |url=https://lkcnhm.nus.edu.sg/wp-content/uploads/sites/10/app/uploads/2017/06/64rbz076-078.pdf |journal=[[Raffles Bulletin of Zoology]] |volume=64 |page=76 |access-date=2022-04-09 |archive-date=2022-03-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220304190818/https://lkcnhm.nus.edu.sg/wp-content/uploads/sites/10/app/uploads/2017/06/64rbz076-078.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Breeding ===
The breeding season of Whitehead's broadbill probably lasts from March to June.<ref name=":0" /> Nests are suspended from slender branches around {{cvt|15|m|ft|-1}} above the ground, with the outside made of fresh green moss. The inside is firm and lined with dry bamboo leaves, forming a well-sheltered cavity. A long extending "tail" made of [[lichen]] and moss helps [[camouflage]] the nest.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Sharpe |first1=Richard Bowdler |last2=Whitehead |first2=John |date=1888 |title=On the Ornithology of Northern Borneo |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/55019#page/465/mode/1up |journal=[[Ibis (journal)|Ibis]] |volume=30 |issue=2 |pages=436–437 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1888.tb07739.x |issn=0019-1019 |lccn=79010132 |oclc=1377260 |via=[[Biodiversity Heritage Library]] |access-date=2022-04-09 |archive-date=2022-03-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220306082902/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/55019#page/465/mode/1up |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Clutch (eggs)|Clutches]] contain one or two eggs,<ref name=":0" /> which are glossy and pale yellow in colour. The eggs measure {{cvt|1.26–1.45 × 1|in|mm}}.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Oates |first1=Eugene William |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/34487#page/188/mode/1up |title=Catalogue of the Collection of Birds' Eggs in the British Museum |last2=Ogilvie-Grant |first2=William Robert |last3=Reid |first3=Philip Savile Grey |date=1903 |volume=3 |publisher= |location=London |pages=160–161 |language=en |doi=10.5962/bhl.title.8298 |oclc=8733662 |via=[[Biodiversity Heritage Library]] |doi-access=free |access-date=2022-04-09 |archive-date=2022-03-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220307105349/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/34487#page/188/mode/1up |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Egg incubation|Incubation]] lasts 19 days and [[fledging]] period is 27 days.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Şahin Arslan |first1=Necmiye |last2=Muñoz |first2=Daniel |last3=Martin |first3=Thomas E. |date=2023-04-03 |title=Reproductive biology of the Whitehead's Broadbill (''Calyptomena whiteheadi'')  |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |volume=123 |issue=2 |pages=152–160 |doi=10.1080/01584197.2023.2193900 |bibcode=2023EmuAO.123..152S |issn=0158-4197}}</ref>

== Status ==
Whitehead's broadbill is classified as being of [[Least-concern species|least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] due to its sufficiently large range and population and a lack of significant population decline.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> It is fairly common in appropriate habitat and occurs in several [[protected area]]s, such as [[Gunung Mulu National Park]] and Kinabalu Park.<ref name=":0" /> However, its population is currently thought to be declining due to [[habitat destruction]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

zh.extract:
黑喉绿阔嘴鸟（学名：Calyptomena whiteheadi）是阔嘴鸟科绿阔嘴鸟属的一种，分布于马来西亚和印度尼西亚。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
黑喉绿阔嘴鸟的平均体重约为156.5克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的湿润低地林和亚热带或热带的湿润山地林。


参考文献

Calyptomena whiteheadi. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27]. （原始内容存档于2014-03-02）.
